concerns.analysis: The image manifest indicates that almost all of the images extracted from the document have 'valid: false' due to a missing Python module ('No module named \'PIL\''). This includes critical images such as the agency logo on page 1, the 'Potential Exposure Pathways' flowchart on page 6, the map of North Carolina on page 28, and numerous screenshots of the Excel tool throughout the document. Because the base64 data is corrupt, these images will appear broken and fail to render in a browser.
concerns.source_pdf_quote: Agency logo on page 1, flowchart on page 6, map on page 28, and numerous software screenshots throughout.
concerns.output_quote: The image manifest shows 'valid': false with the error 'No module named \'PIL\'' for almost all images, meaning the base64 data is corrupt and the images will be broken when rendered.

concerns.description: Link text pulled over but not hyperlinked
concerns.analysis: Across multiple pages, text that is a clickable hyperlink in the source PDF is extracted as plain text. The model has fragmented the sentences containing these links into multiple paragraphs, isolating the link text into its own paragraph but failing to wrap it in an anchor (`<a href="...">`) tag.
concerns.source_pdf_quote: "Refer to the Risk Evaluation Resources page on the NCDEQ Risk-Based Remediation website..." (where "Risk Evaluation Resources" is a clickable hyperlink)
concerns.output_quote: The text is split into three paragraphs, with the hyperlink completely lost: {"type": "paragraph", "text": "Risk Evaluation Resources"}

concerns.description: Header/footer elements retained in output (69): Risk Calculator User Guide; Risk Calculator User Guide; February 2024
concerns.analysis: header_footer_count=69. Header/footer text from extraction JSON is present in the rendered HTML — the renderer failed to strip it. This is a structural accessibility issue.

concerns.description: Footnote(s) converted to bulleted list: 1 unordered list(s) after table(s) appear to be footnotes
concerns.analysis: footnote_as_list_count=1. Examples: 1 item(s): "Chemical with a Henry's Law constant less than or ". Table footnotes (often marked with *, †, ‡) were converted to <ul><li> elements instead of paragraphs. This changes the semantic meaning — screen readers announce them as list items rather than explanatory notes.

concerns.description: Table header markup issues: 1 table(s) have a data row marked as header: "ASTM"
concerns.analysis: data_in_th=0, datarow_as_header=1. Tables use <th> for data values (misrepresenting data as headers) or have a data row mismarked as a header row. Screen readers rely on <th> to announce column/row context.

The source document uses underlined text for clickable hyperlinks (e.g., 'Risk Evaluation Resources' on page 5). The extraction output completely loses this underline emphasis, rendering the text as standard paragraph text without any markdown or formatting indicators for the underline/link.

concerns.description: 2 bare URL(s) in text not converted to hyperlinks: *janet.macdonald@deq.nc.gov; *david.lilley@deq.nc.gov
concerns.analysis: bare_url_count=2. URLs appear as plain text instead of clickable links.

concerns.description: Table header markup issues: 1 table(s) with 3+ rows have no <th> header cells
concerns.analysis: data_in_th_time=0, no_th_tables=1. Minor semantic markup issues: tables lack <th> elements or use time values as headers. These affect screen reader context but are less severe than data misrepresentation.

concerns.description: Source PDF contains 21 instance(s) of blue-styled text without hyperlink annotations. These appear to be intended as hyperlinks but were not annotated in the PDF (likely lost during Word-to-PDF export). This is a source document limitation, not a pipeline defect. Examples: page 5: "Risk Evaluation Resources"; page 5: "USEPA Regional Screening Level"; page 5: "(RSL) Tables"; page 5: "Technical"; page 5: "Guidance for Risk-Based Environmental Remediation "
concerns.analysis: orphaned_blue_text_count=21. Blue text without link annotations detected via PyMuPDF color extraction.
